content:
𝗠𝗚𝗟'𝘀 𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗴𝗶𝗻 𝗳𝗲𝗹𝗹 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 ~𝟯𝟬% 𝘁𝗼 ~𝟭𝟴%


"At first, I thought..."Okay, the business must be struggling.

Then I spent some time reading Mahanagar Gas Limited's (MGL) Annual Report, earnings calls, and analysing its financials on Screener.

And I realised something interesting.

MGL isn't one 

business, it's three different businesses operating under one roof!

.🚗 𝗖𝗡𝗚 (𝟳𝟮% 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗼𝘁𝗮𝗹 𝘃𝗼𝗹𝘂𝗺𝗲𝘀):


This is MGL's biggest revenue driver. While Mumbai has started showing signs of maturity with slower growth due to EV adoption and limited station expansion, the next phase of growth is coming from Thane, Raigad, commercial fleet conversions, and acquisitions like UEPL.

🏠 𝗗𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗰 𝗣𝗡𝗚:

A slow but consistent business with over 3.2 million household connections. Growth is driven by adding new customers rather than increasing prices. However, because this segment relies entirely on government-allocated APM gas, profitability is heavily influenced by policy decisions and changes in gas prices.

🏭 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗹 & 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗲𝗿𝗰𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗣𝗡𝗚

: The smallest but most unpredictable segment. Although demand remained healthy through most of FY26, Q4 volumes were impacted by LNG supply disruptions caused by geopolitical tensions in West Asia. 

Since this business is linked to Brent crude prices, its earnings behave very differently from the other two segments.

That's when the margin story finally clicked.The fall in operating margins wasn't because one business was underperforming.

It was because three different challenges were playing out simultaneously:

• 𝗖𝗡𝗚 𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗴𝗶𝗻𝘀 𝘄𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗯𝘆
𝗵𝗶𝗴𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗴𝗮𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝘀𝘁𝘀.

• 𝗗𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗰 𝗣𝗡𝗚 faced regulatory pricing pressure as APM gas costs increased.

• 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗹 & 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗲𝗿𝗰𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗣𝗡𝗚 was affected by LNG supply disruptions and global commodity prices.The headline number was the same.

But the reasons behind it were
completely different.

Screener helps me identify what
changed.

Annual reports and earnings calls help me understand why it changed.
And I've realised that the real insights are often hidden beyond the financial statements.

Have you come across a company that looked completely different once you broke it down segment by segment?

 I'd love to hear your thoughts.

This is DAY (41/100) of
